content:
# Exodus Chapter 22 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~45r–45v (PDF pp. 88–89); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 64–65 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_022_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_022_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Laws Concerning Theft (vv. 1–4)

**1** If a man [**ki yignov ish**] steals [**yignov**] an ox [**shor**] or a sheep [**o-seh**] and slaughters it [**utvacho**] or sells it [**o mekharo**], five oxen [**chamishah vaqar**] he shall pay [**yeshallem**] for the ox [**tachat hashshor**], and four sheep [**ve'arba tson**] for the sheep [**tachat hasseh**].

**2** If the thief [**im-bamachteret**] is found [**yimmatse**] breaking in [**bamachteret**] and is struck [**vehukkah**] and dies [**vamet**], there is no bloodguilt [**eyn lo damim**] for him.

**3** If the sun [**im-zarchah hashshemesh**] has risen [**zarchah**] upon him [**alav**], there is bloodguilt [**damim lo**] for him. He shall surely make restitution [**shallem yeshallem**]; if he has nothing [**im-eyn lo**], then he shall be sold [**venimkar**] for his theft [**bignevato**].

**4** If the stolen animal [**im-himmatse timmatse veyado**] is found [**himmatse timmatse**] alive [**chayyim**] in his hand [**veyado**], whether ox [**mishor**] or donkey [**ad-chamor**] or sheep [**ad-seh**], double [**shenayim**] he shall pay [**yeshallem**].

---

## Laws Concerning Agricultural Damage (vv. 5–6)

**5** When a man [**ki yav'er ish**] causes a field [**sadeh**] or vineyard [**o-kerem**] to be grazed over [**yav'er**] and lets loose [**veshillach**] his livestock [**et-be'iro**] and it grazes [**uvi'er**] in another man's field [**bisdeh acher**], the best [**meytav**] of his own field [**sadehu**] and the best [**umeytav**] of his own vineyard [**karmo**] he shall make restitution [**yeshallem**].

**6** When fire [**ki-tetse esh**] breaks out [**tetse**] and catches [**umatse'ah**] thorns [**qotsim**], and a stack of grain [**gaddish**] or the standing grain [**o haqamah**] or the field [**o hassadeh**] is consumed [**vene'ekhal**], the one who kindled the fire [**hamav'ir et-habe'erah**] shall surely make restitution [**shallem yeshallem**].

---

## Laws Concerning Deposits and Bailment (vv. 7–13)

**7** When a man [**ki-yitten ish**] gives [**yitten**] to his neighbor [**el-re'ehu**] money [**kesef**] or goods [**o-khelim**] to keep [**lishmor**], and it is stolen [**vegunnav**] from the man's house [**mibbeyt ha'ish**] — if the thief [**im-yimmatse haggannav**] is found [**yimmatse**], he shall pay [**yeshallem**] double [**shenayim**].

**8** If the thief [**im-lo yimmatse haggannav**] is not found [**lo yimmatse**], then the owner of the house [**ba'al habbayit**] shall come near [**veniqrav**] to God [**el-ha'Elohim**], to determine whether or not [**im-lo**] he laid [**shalach**] his hand [**yado**] upon his neighbor's property [**bimle'khet re'ehu**].

**9** For every matter [**al-kol-devar**] of transgression [**pesha**] — for an ox [**al-shor**], for a donkey [**al-chamor**], for a sheep [**al-seh**], for a garment [**al-salmah**], for any lost thing [**al-kol-avedah**] of which one says [**asher yomar**], 'This is it [**ki hu zeh**]' — the case [**devar**] of both of them [**sheneyhem**] shall come [**yavo**] before God [**ad-ha'Elohim**]; whomever [**asher**] God [**Elohim**] declares guilty [**yarshi'un**] shall pay [**yeshallem**] double [**shenayim**] to his neighbor [**lere'ehu**].

**10** When a man [**ki-yitten ish**] gives [**yitten**] to his neighbor [**el-re'ehu**] a donkey [**chamor**] or an ox [**o-shor**] or a sheep [**o-seh**] or any animal [**vekhol-behemah**] to keep [**lishmor**], and it dies [**vamet**] or is injured [**o-nishbar**] or is driven away [**o-nishbah**] with no one seeing [**eyn ro'eh**] —

**11** an oath [**shevu'at**] of YHWH [**YHWH**] shall be between them both [**tihyeh beyn sheneyhem**], to determine whether or not [**im-lo**] he laid [**shalach**] his hand [**yado**] upon his neighbor's property [**bimle'khet re'ehu**]; and its owner [**uva'alav**] shall accept [**velaqach**] it, and he shall not make restitution [**velo yeshallem**].

**12** But if it is indeed stolen [**ve'im-ganov yigganev**] from him [**me'immo**], he shall make restitution [**yeshallem**] to its owner [**liv'alav**].

**13** If it is indeed torn by a beast [**im-tarof yittaref**], let him bring it [**yevi'ehu**] as evidence [**ed**]; he shall not make restitution [**lo yeshallem**] for that which was torn [**hattrefah**].

---

## Laws Concerning Borrowed Property (vv. 14–15)

**14** And when a man [**vechi-yish'al ish**] borrows [**yish'al**] from his neighbor [**me'im re'ehu**] and it is injured [**venishbar**] or dies [**o-met**], its owner [**be'alav**] not being with it [**eyn-immo**], he shall surely make restitution [**shallem yeshallem**].

**15** If its owner [**im-be'alav**] is with it [**immo**], he shall not make restitution [**lo yeshallem**]; if it is hired [**im-sakhir hu**], it came [**ba**] for its hire [**biskharo**].

---

## Law Concerning Seduction of a Virgin (vv. 16–17)

**16** And when a man [**vechi yefatteh ish**] seduces [**yefatteh**] a virgin [**betulah**] who is not betrothed [**asher lo-orasah**] and lies [**veshakhav**] with her [**immah**], he shall surely pay [**mahor yimharenah**] the bride-price [**mahor**] for her to be his wife [**lo le'ishah**].

**17** If her father [**im-ma'en yema'en aviha**] utterly refuses [**ma'en yema'en**] to give her [**letittah**] to him [**lo**], money [**kesef**] he shall weigh out [**yishqol**] according to the bride-price [**kemohar**] of virgins [**habetulot**].

---

## Capital and Apodictic Laws (vv. 18–20)

**18** You shall not allow a sorceress [**mekhashefah**] to live [**lo techayeh**].

**19** Anyone who lies with [**kol-shokhev**] an animal [**im-behemah**] shall surely be put to death [**mot yumat**].

**20** Whoever sacrifices [**zoveach**] to any gods [**la'elohim**] shall be utterly destroyed [**yochoram**], except [**bilti**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**] alone [**levaddo**].

---

## Laws Protecting the Vulnerable (vv. 21–24)

**21** And a sojourner [**veger**] you shall not wrong [**lo-toneh**] and you shall not oppress him [**velo tilchatsennu**], for sojourners [**ki-gerim**] you were [**heyitem**] in the land of [**be'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**].

**22** Any widow [**kol-almanah**] or orphan [**veyatom**] you shall not afflict [**lo te'annun**].

**23** If you indeed afflict him [**im-anneh te'anneh**] — for if [**ki im**] he cries out [**tsa'oq yits'aq**] to Me [**elay**], I will surely hear [**shamoa eshma**] his cry [**tsa'aqato**] —

**24** then My anger [**vecharah appi**] shall burn [**vecharah**], and I will kill [**veharagti**] you [**etkhem**] with the sword [**becherev**], and your wives [**vehyu nesheykhem**] shall become [**vehyu**] widows [**almanot**] and your children [**uvneykhem**] orphans [**yetomim**].

---

## Laws Concerning Lending (vv. 25–27)

**25** If you lend money [**im-kesef talveh**] to My people [**et-ammi**], to the poor [**et-he'ani**] who is with you [**immakh**], you shall not be [**lo-tihyeh lo**] to him as a creditor [**kenosheh**]; you shall not lay [**lo-tesimun**] upon him [**alav**] interest [**neshekh**].

**26** If you indeed take as pledge [**im-chavol tachbol**] the garment [**salmat**] of your neighbor [**re'ekha**], before the sun goes down [**ad-bo hashshemesh**] you shall return it [**teshivennu**] to him [**lo**].

**27** For it [**ki hi**] is his only [**levaddah**] covering [**khesuto**]; it [**hi**] is his garment [**simlato**] for his skin [**le'oro**]. In what [**bammeh**] shall he sleep [**yishkav**]? And it shall be [**vehayah**], when he cries out [**ki-yits'aq**] to Me [**elay**], that I will hear [**veshama'ti**], for I [**ki**] am gracious [**channun ani**].

---

## Laws Concerning Reverence and Firstfruits (vv. 28–31)

**28** God [**Elohim**] you shall not revile [**lo teqallel**], and a ruler [**venasi**] among your people [**ve'ammekha**] you shall not curse [**lo ta'or**].

**29** Your fullness [**mele'atkha**] and your overflow [**vedim'akha**] you shall not delay [**lo te'acher**]. The firstborn [**bekhor**] of your sons [**banekha**] you shall give [**titten**] to Me [**li**].

**30** Likewise [**ken-ta'aseh**] you shall do [**ta'aseh**] with your ox [**leshorekha**] and with your sheep [**letsonekha**]: seven days [**shiv'at yamim**] it shall be [**yihyeh**] with its mother [**im-immo**]; on the eighth day [**bayyom hashemini**] you shall give it [**tittneno**] to Me [**li**].

**31** And men [**ve'anshey**] of holiness [**qodesh**] you shall be [**tihyun**] to Me [**li**]. And flesh [**uvasar**] in the field [**bassadeh**] torn by beasts [**trefah**] you shall not eat [**lo tokhelu**]; to the dogs [**lakkelev**] you shall cast [**tashlikhun**] it [**oto**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**machteret** (v. 2) — "breaking in, tunneling." From the root ch-t-r ("to dig through"), machteret denotes the act of breaching a wall or barrier to gain entry — tunneling through the mud-brick walls typical of ancient Near Eastern houses. The legal logic of vv. 2-3 turns on the time of day: a nighttime break-in (v. 2) creates a presumption that the intruder may intend lethal violence, and the householder who kills him incurs no bloodguilt (eyn lo damim, literally "there is no blood to him"). But a daytime break-in (v. 3, "if the sun has risen upon him") removes this presumption — the intruder can be seen and identified, the householder can summon help, and therefore killing is disproportionate. The LXX renders machteret as diorugma ("a dig-through"), preserving the etymological sense. The distinction between nighttime and daytime self-defense is one of the earliest articulations of proportionality in the use of lethal force.

**ha'Elohim** (vv. 8–9) — "God" or "the judges." As in 21:6, the term ha'Elohim in a juridical context may designate either God Himself or human judges who render decisions in God's name. In v. 8, the householder accused of misappropriating entrusted property must "come near to ha'Elohim" — either approach the sanctuary for a divine adjudication (an ordeal or oath) or appear before authorized judges. In v. 9, the disputed claim "shall come before ha'Elohim," and "whomever Elohim declares guilty (yarshi'un)" shall pay double. The plural verb yarshi'un ("they condemn") with the singular-looking Elohim has long been noted: it may support the "judges" interpretation (a true plural subject), or it may be an honorific plural with a singular divine referent. The LXX renders v. 8 as enopion tou theou ("before God") and v. 9 as the parallel enopion tou theou, preserving the divine reference. The formal equivalence rendering retains "God" as the primary meaning while acknowledging the judicial overlay.

**shevu'at YHWH** (v. 11) — "an oath of YHWH." This is the only occurrence in the Torah of a sworn oath explicitly designated by the divine name in the context of a property dispute. The oath is not a private promise but a sacral act invoking the covenant God as witness and enforcer. The one who swears falsely by YHWH's name brings the curse of the oath upon himself. The LXX renders this as horkos tou theou ("oath of God"), substituting the generic theos for the Tetragrammaton, as is standard Septuagintal practice. The legal function of the oath is to close a case that cannot be resolved by evidence: when there is "no one seeing" (eyn ro'eh, v. 10), the oath transfers the matter from the human to the divine court.

**mekhashefah** (v. 18) — "sorceress." The feminine participle from the Piel of k-sh-f. The root kashaf refers to the practice of sorcery, incantation, or manipulative ritual — an attempt to control divine or supernatural forces through techniques rather than through prayer and covenant faithfulness. The feminine form is used here, but Deuteronomy 18:10 prohibits the practice for both sexes. The famous King James rendering "Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live" derives from this verse. The LXX renders mekhashefah as pharmakous ("sorcerers/poisoners"), using the masculine plural — broadening the prohibition beyond the feminine singular of the MT. The root pharmak- in Greek encompasses both ritual sorcery and the preparation of potions and poisons, a semantic range that influenced later Roman and medieval law's conflation of witchcraft with poisoning.

**cherem** (v. 20) — "utterly destroyed, devoted to destruction." The Hophal yochoram ("shall be put under the ban") derives from the root ch-r-m, one of the most theologically weighty terms in the Hebrew Bible. The cherem is not ordinary capital punishment but sacral destruction — the complete dedication (and therefore annihilation) of a person or thing to YHWH. What is placed under cherem is removed entirely from the human sphere. The same term governs the destruction of the Canaanite cities (Deut 7:2; Josh 6:17). The LXX renders yochoram as olethreuthēsetai ("shall be utterly destroyed"), capturing the totality but losing the sacral-dedicatory nuance of the Hebrew. The verse creates a sharp theological boundary: sacrifice to any deity other than YHWH alone (levaddo) places the offender in the same category as the hostile nations devoted to destruction.

**ger** (v. 21) — "sojourner, resident alien." The ger is a person who dwells in a community not his own, lacking the protections of kinship, land ownership, and tribal affiliation. The ger is distinguished from the nokhri ("foreigner, stranger passing through") and the toshav ("temporary settler"). The ger has entered the community with some degree of permanence and is entitled to legal protection. The motivation clause — "for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt" (ki-gerim heyitem be'erets Mitsrayim) — is the first occurrence of what becomes one of the most frequently repeated ethical arguments in the Torah (cf. Exod 23:9; Lev 19:34; Deut 10:19). Israel's own experience of alienation and oppression in Egypt is the moral foundation for its treatment of the vulnerable. The LXX renders ger as prosēluton ("proselyte"), a translation that shifts the meaning from ethnic-social outsider to religious convert — a shift with enormous consequences for later Jewish and Christian understandings of the term.

**neshekh** (v. 25) — "interest, usury." Literally "a bite" — from the root n-sh-kh ("to bite"). The metaphor is vivid: interest "bites" into the borrower's substance, consuming his resources. The prohibition against interest applies specifically to loans made to "My people, the poor who are with you" — it is not a blanket prohibition on all commercial lending but a protection for the vulnerable within the covenant community. The LXX renders neshekh as tokos ("offspring, interest"), using the Greek financial term that imagines money "breeding" money — a different metaphor from the Hebrew's predatory "bite." The prohibition of interest on loans to the poor is one of the foundational texts for the later rabbinic and Islamic prohibitions on usury and for medieval Christian debates about the morality of lending at interest.

**channun** (v. 27) — "gracious." From the root ch-n-n ("to be gracious, show favor"), channun is one of the divine self-descriptions that will be expanded in the great theophanic formula of Exodus 34:6 (YHWH, YHWH, El rachum vechanun — "YHWH, YHWH, a God merciful and gracious"). Here, in the context of the pledge-law, YHWH declares "I am gracious" (channun ani) as the ground for hearing the cry of the poor man who shivers without his garment. The divine graciousness is not abstract benevolence but concrete responsiveness to the cry of the oppressed. The first-person declaration — "I will hear, for I am gracious" — makes God Himself the enforcer of the lending laws, transforming a civil regulation into a theological claim about the divine character.

**mele'ah vedim'ah** (v. 29) — "fullness and overflow." This compressed expression refers to the produce of field and vineyard: mele'ah ("fullness") denotes the ripe grain harvest, and dim'ah ("overflow, tears, dripping") denotes the juice that flows from pressed grapes or olives. The terms are metonymic — the fullness of the threshing floor and the overflow of the winepress or oil-vat. The LXX renders this as aparchas halōnos kai lēnou ("firstfruits of the threshing-floor and the wine-press"), making the metonymy explicit. The command "you shall not delay" (lo te'acher) demands that these firstfruits be offered promptly, without holding back — the first and best belong to YHWH before any human consumption.

### Grammatical Notes

**shallem yeshallem** (vv. 3, 6, 14) — The Piel infinitive absolute with Piel imperfect, repeated throughout this chapter as the standard formula for mandatory restitution. The double emphasis ("he shall surely make restitution, he shall certainly pay in full") underscores the non-negotiable character of the obligation. The Piel stem of sh-l-m means "to make whole, restore completeness" — the offender must restore the victim to the state of wholeness (shalom) that existed before the offense.

**im-anneh te'anneh** (v. 23) — "if you indeed afflict." The Piel infinitive absolute with Piel imperfect of 'a-n-h ("to afflict, oppress"). The emphatic construction intensifies the warning: any affliction whatsoever. The verse then shifts abruptly to the divine first person: "for if he cries out to Me, I will surely hear his cry" (ki im-tsa'oq yits'aq elay shamoa eshma tsa'aqato). The stacking of emphatic infinitive-absolute constructions — te'anneh (you afflict), yits'aq (he cries), eshma (I hear) — creates a chain of cause and effect: your oppression triggers his cry, his cry triggers My response. Three emphatic verbs, three actors (oppressor, victim, God), an escalating sequence that ends in divine wrath (v. 24).

**ma'en yema'en** (v. 17) — "utterly refuses, refuses absolutely." The Piel infinitive absolute with Piel imperfect of m-'-n ("to refuse"). The emphatic form indicates a resolute, categorical refusal — the father does not merely hesitate but positively and firmly declines to give his daughter to the seducer. Even in this case, the seducer must still pay the bride-price (mohar), though he does not receive the wife. The payment functions as both penalty and compensation for the diminished marriageability of the daughter.

**chamol tachbol** (v. 26) — "if you indeed take as pledge." The infinitive absolute with imperfect of ch-b-l ("to take in pledge, bind"). The verb chaval has an interesting semantic range: it can mean "to bind" (as in binding a debtor with obligation), "to take as pledge," and in other stems, "to damage, ruin, act corruptly." The moral undertone is that taking a poor man's garment as pledge is itself a kind of ruin — reducing him to nakedness and cold. The command to return it before sunset transforms the pledge from a seizure into a temporary, almost symbolic act: the creditor may hold the garment during the day (as a reminder of the debt) but must return it each evening so the debtor can sleep in warmth.

**yarshi'un** (v. 9) — "declares guilty, condemns." The Hiphil imperfect third-person masculine plural of r-sh-' ("to be wicked, guilty"). The plural verb form with Elohim is grammatically notable. If Elohim here means "God" (singular), the plural verb is anomalous — though not without parallel (cf. Gen 20:13; 2 Sam 7:23). If Elohim means "judges" (a functional plural), the plural verb is natural. The formal equivalence rendering translates "God declares guilty" while noting the grammatical ambiguity. The Hiphil stem is causative-declarative: "to declare (someone) to be in the wrong" — a forensic verdict, not a moral transformation.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 22 continues the Book of the Covenant (sefer habberit) that began at 21:1 and will conclude at 23:33. The chapter exhibits a discernible structural progression from property laws (vv. 1-15) through personal-sexual law (vv. 16-17) to capital religious offenses (vv. 18-20) and finally to social-ethical commands with theological grounding (vv. 21-31). This movement — from property to persons to God — mirrors the theological logic of the entire Sinai legislation: material concerns are not dismissed but are progressively situated within a framework of covenant relationship and divine holiness.

The property-law section (vv. 1-15) is the most fully developed casuistic legislation in the chapter. It addresses theft (vv. 1-4), agricultural damage (vv. 5-6), deposit and bailment (vv. 7-13), and borrowed property (vv. 14-15). The cases move from outright theft to negligence to disputed claims, requiring progressively more sophisticated legal mechanisms — from simple restitution to oath-taking before God. The restitution scales are calibrated to the offense: five-fold for an ox (a capital asset, a means of production), four-fold for a sheep (a lesser capital asset), and two-fold for goods found still in the thief's possession (where the owner's loss is less).

The transitional section (vv. 16-20) moves from casuistic ("if/when") to apodictic ("you shall not") form. The seduction law (vv. 16-17) is the last casuistic case; the sorcery prohibition (v. 18), the bestiality law (v. 19), and the idolatry law (v. 20) are all apodictic — absolute prohibitions without conditional framing. This shift in legal form marks a shift in legal gravity: the property cases admit of degrees, nuances, and monetary settlements, but sorcery, bestiality, and false worship admit of no mitigation. They strike at the foundations of the covenant order.

The final section (vv. 21-31) is entirely apodictic and increasingly theological. The laws protecting sojourners, widows, and orphans (vv. 21-24) are grounded not in casuistic reasoning but in Israel's historical experience ("for sojourners you were in Egypt") and in the divine character ("I am gracious"). The lending laws (vv. 25-27) are enforced not by judges but by God Himself ("when he cries out to Me, I will hear"). The concluding commands about reverence for God (v. 28), prompt offering of firstfruits (v. 29), consecration of the firstborn (v. 30), and dietary holiness (v. 31) bring the chapter to its theological climax: "men of holiness you shall be to Me." The chapter that began with stolen oxen ends with a call to sacred identity. The legal and the holy are not separate domains but a single continuum.

### Theological Notes

The restitution laws of Exodus 22:1-15 embody a distinctive theology of property. Property in ancient Israel is not an absolute individual right but a trust held under God's sovereignty. The thief who steals an ox does not merely violate the owner's property right; he disrupts the order of shalom that God intends for the covenant community. The remedy is not simply return but restoration with increase — the thief must pay back more than he took (five-fold, four-fold, or double), because the offense is not only material but relational. The surplus in the restitution payment is the cost of healing the breach. This principle — that restoration requires more than mere return — runs through biblical theology from the restitution laws to the Servant Songs of Isaiah to the New Testament concept of redemption.

The self-defense law (vv. 2-3) is remarkable for its theological restraint. The Torah, which elsewhere demands "life for life" (21:23), here acknowledges a space where taking life is justified — but only within strict limits. The nighttime intruder may be killed without guilt; the daytime intruder may not. The distinction is not about the intruder's intent (which may be identical in both cases) but about the defender's ability to assess the situation and respond proportionately. Where discernment is possible, lethal force is not permitted. This is law shaped by the conviction that human life — even the life of a thief — has value before God.

The oath of YHWH (v. 11) is a remarkable intersection of legal procedure and theology. When evidence fails — "no one seeing" (eyn ro'eh, v. 10) — the case is transferred from the human court to the divine court through a sworn oath. The underlying assumption is that YHWH sees what no human witness saw. The oath is not a legal fiction or a formality; it is an invocation of divine omniscience as the court of last resort. The one who swears falsely by YHWH's name does not escape justice; he merely shifts his judgment from the human to the divine tribunal, where the penalty is far more severe. This legal-theological principle undergirds the entire covenant system: human justice is always embedded within, and answerable to, divine justice.

The protection of the vulnerable (vv. 21-24) represents one of the Torah's most distinctive ethical contributions. The sojourner, the widow, and the orphan are the paradigmatic vulnerable persons in the ancient Near East — those who lack the social networks (kinship, land, husband, father) that provide security and standing. The Torah does not simply recommend compassion toward them; it commands their protection with the force of law and enforces that command with a divine threat of lethal severity (v. 24: "I will kill you with the sword, and your wives shall become widows and your children orphans"). The punishment is measure-for-measure: those who make widows and orphans of others will have their own families reduced to the same condition. The theological logic is that YHWH Himself occupies the role of kinsman-protector (go'el) for the defenseless — what they lack in human patrons, He supplies in divine advocacy.

The chapter's concluding verse — "men of holiness you shall be to Me" (ve'anshey-qodesh tihyun li, v. 31) — is the interpretive key to the entire legislative section. The dietary restriction that follows (do not eat flesh torn by beasts; cast it to the dogs) is not arbitrary; it is the concrete expression of a holy identity. Holiness in the Torah is not primarily a spiritual or mystical category but a practical one: it is lived out in what you eat, how you lend, when you return a pledge, and how you treat the stranger. The shift from "holy nation" (goy qadosh, 19:6) to "men of holiness" (anshey-qodesh, 22:31) is a shift from corporate identity to individual responsibility: each Israelite, in each concrete act of daily life, either embodies or betrays the holiness to which he is called.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 22 (pages 64-65) preserves the LXX version with several notable features and divergences from the MT. The LXX verse numbering for this chapter differs from the MT: what is Exodus 22:1 in the MT is numbered as 21:37 in the LXX tradition, with the LXX's chapter 22 beginning at MT 22:2. This numbering discrepancy persists throughout the chapter, with the LXX consistently one verse behind the MT. At v. 8 (MT), the critical ha'Elohim is rendered enopion tou theou ("before God"), maintaining the divine rather than judicial interpretation. At v. 9, the LXX similarly has enopion tou theou for the forensic appearance. At v. 11, shevu'at YHWH becomes horkos tou theou ("oath of God"), with the standard Septuagintal substitution of theos for the Tetragrammaton. At v. 18, the MT's feminine singular mekhashefah ("sorceress") is rendered in Vaticanus as pharmakous (masculine plural, "sorcerers/poisoners"), broadening the gender scope of the prohibition — a reading that may reflect the translators' interpretive decision to make the law universally applicable rather than gender-specific. At v. 20, the MT's yochoram ("shall be devoted to destruction") is rendered olethreuthēsetai ("shall be utterly destroyed"), capturing the finality but losing the sacral-dedicatory dimension of the cherem. At v. 21, the MT's ger ("sojourner") is rendered prosēluton ("proselyte"), reflecting the Hellenistic-era shift in understanding the ger as a religious convert rather than merely a resident alien. At v. 25, the MT's neshekh ("interest, bite") is rendered ep' tokō ("at interest"), using the standard Greek financial term. At v. 28, the MT's Elohim lo teqallel ("God you shall not revile") is rendered theous ou kakologēseis ("gods you shall not revile"), with the plural theous — which may reflect an understanding of Elohim as a true plural here (referring to judges or dignitaries), or it may preserve an older reading in which the prohibition extends to reviling even foreign deities (cf. the similar interpretation in Josephus, Ant. 4.207, and Philo, Vit. Mos. 2.205). At v. 29, the MT's compressed mele'atkha vedim'akha ("your fullness and your overflow") is rendered aparchas halōnos sou kai lēnou sou ("the firstfruits of your threshing-floor and your wine-press"), making the metonymic referents explicit. At v. 31, the MT's ve'anshey-qodesh tihyun li ("men of holiness you shall be to Me") is rendered kai andres hagioi esesthe moi ("and holy men you shall be to Me"), rendering anshey-qodesh as a simple adjective-noun phrase rather than preserving the construct-chain structure. Throughout Vaticanus pages 64-65, the Exodus 22 text is written in continuous uncial script in three columns, with the chapter beginning in the lower portion of page 64 (following the conclusion of chapter 21) and continuing through approximately the middle of page 65, where it transitions into chapter 23. The text is well-preserved with minimal lacunae in this section.

--- LOGOS
